message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it. Read the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and find the code path / contract / test that makes the PoC impossible or the concern moot. Only conclude survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

Finding T-2: test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted
File: tests/test_observability.py:162
Description: The test name and intent assert that BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired (the #O3 fix: atexit does not run on `kill`/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler is also installed to flush spans). But the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:` (lines 188-190). `captured["h"]` is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling `signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler)` (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the entire SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-142) and keeps only `atexit.register(...)` (line 130), then `signal.signal` is never invoked, `captured` stays empty, the `if "h" in captured` branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. So the SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is supposed to catch — the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.
PoC: In src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py, delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (lines 131-142), keeping `atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))`. `test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm` still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; `captured` is empty so the `if "h" in captured` SIGTERM assertions are skipped.

Return survives=false if you can refute it (with the specific reason), survives=true if it withstands a genuine disproof attempt.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

162	def test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm(monkeypatch):
163	    # span-flush on exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM must be wired and call force_flush/shutdown,
164	    # else buffered provenance spans are lost on container stop. No backend needed.
165	    import signal as _signal
166	
167	    calls = {"flush": 0, "shutdown": 0, "atexit": []}
168	
169	    class _Provider:
170	        def force_flush(self):
171	            calls["flush"] += 1
172	
173	        def shutdown(self):
174	            calls["shutdown"] += 1
175	
176	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.atexit, "register", lambda fn: calls["atexit"].append(fn))
177	    captured = {}
178	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.signal, "getsignal", lambda s: _signal.SIG_DFL)
179	    monkeypatch.setattr(
180	        tr.signal, "signal", lambda sig, handler: captured.__setitem__("h", handler)
181	    )
182	
183	    tr._install_flush_on_exit(_Provider())
184	
185	    assert calls["atexit"], "atexit flush handler not registered"
186	    calls["atexit"][0]()  # simulate interpreter exit
187	    assert calls["flush"] >= 1
188	    if "h" in captured:  # SIGTERM handler installed (main thread)
189	        captured["h"](_signal.SIGTERM, None)
190	        assert calls["shutdown"] >= 1

116	        LangChainInstrumentor().instrument(skip_dep_check=True)

120	
121	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
122	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
123	
124	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
125	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
126	    """
127	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
128	    if not callable(flush):
129	        return
130	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
131	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
132	        try:
133	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
134	
135	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
136	                _safe(flush)
137	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
138	                if callable(prior):
139	                    prior(signum, frame)
140	
141	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
142	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
143	            pass
144	
145	
146	def _safe(fn: Any) -> None:
147	    try:
148	        fn()
149	    except Exception:
150	        pass
151	
152	
153	@contextmanager
154	def run_trace_context(run_id: str | None) -> Iterator[None]:

content.text:
The finding is correct. The test runs in the main thread (pytest), so `threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()` is True, and `signal.signal` (mocked) gets called, populating `captured["h"]`. The SIGTERM assertions DO run currently.

But the finding's claim is about the *revert*: if a developer deletes lines 131-142, then `signal.signal` is never called, `captured` stays empty, and the `if "h" in captured:` branch is skipped. The test would then pass solely on the atexit assertions (185-187), which still hold. Let me verify the revert scenario by confirming nothing else would fail.

The atexit assertions don't depend on the SIGTERM block at all. After the revert:
- Line 185: `calls["atexit"]` is non-empty (atexit.register still called) — passes
- Line 186-187: flush handler invoked, `calls["flush"] >= 1` — passes
- Line 188: `"h" in captured` is False — branch skipped, no shutdown assertion runs

So the regression guard for the SIGTERM half is vacuous on the exact revert it claims to catch. The conditional `if "h" in captured:` makes the SIGTERM assertion opt-in based on the very behavior under test.

Let me confirm there's no other test covering the SIGTERM wiring that would catch the revert.

content.text:
No other test covers the SIGTERM wiring. The only guard is this one test, and its SIGTERM assertions are gated behind `if "h" in captured:` — which is exactly the side effect the revert removes.

The finding is confirmed: the conditional makes the SIGTERM regression guard vacuous on the precise revert it's supposed to catch.

input.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the actual code. tracing.py:131-141 only calls signal.signal (the mocked setattr at test_observability.py:179-181 that populates captured["h"]) when running on the main thread. The test asserts the SIGTERM behavior (shutdown >= 1) only inside `if "h" in captured:` at line 188. captured["h"] exists *solely* as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal. Under the PoC revert (delete lines 131-142, keep atexit.register at line 130), signal.signal is never invoked, captured stays empty, the `if "h" in captured` branch is skipped, and the remaining assertions (line 185 atexit non-empty, line 187 flush>=1) still pass because atexit.register is untouched. I grepped tests/ for SIGTERM/signal.signal/getsignal coverage: this is the only test touching the SIGTERM path, so nothing else catches the revert. The SIGTERM half of the regression guard is therefore vacuous on exactly the revert it claims to detect. The fix is to make the SIGTERM assertion unconditional (e.g. `assert "h" in captured` before invoking it), since the test runs on pytest's main thread where the handler must be installed.

content.text: The finding survives. The `if "h" in captured:` conditional at test_observability.py:188 makes the SIGTERM assertions opt-in based on the very side effect the revert removes, so the test passes on its atexit assertions alone when the #O3 SIGTERM block is deleted. No other test covers the SIGTERM path.
